title: EU Commission's industrial Action Plan for the European automotive sector
bodyHtml:
In reference to the letter from the Ministry of Trade, the letter dated 14 April 2025 and numbered 999 from the Turkish Exporters Assembly states that, within the scope of the Strategic Dialogue on the Future of the Automotive Industry announced on 30 January 2025, the European Commission released the “Industrial Action Plan for the European Automotive Sector” on 5 March 2025.

It is reported that the highlights of the Action Plan are summarized as follows:

– Under the theme of innovation and digitalization:

Legislative efforts regarding artificial intelligence and autonomous vehicles will be increased. Under the Horizon Europe program, €1 billion in funding will be provided for the automotive sector for the 2025–2027 period, of which €350 million will be allocated to support the EU value chain, including recycling in next-generation battery technologies.

– Under the theme of clean transition and decarbonization:

To boost demand for zero-emission vehicles, it is planned that 60% of corporate fleets will be converted to environmentally friendly alternatives. Funding for charging infrastructure will be increased, and rapid establishment of heavy-duty vehicle charging stations is also planned.

– Under the theme of competitiveness and supply chain security:

€1.8 billion in financing will be provided to companies producing batteries in the EU. State aid rules will be simplified. Efforts will be made to provide financial support for end-of-life vehicles and battery recycling facilities. Furthermore, it is foreseen that “black mass,” a recyclable raw material derived from battery waste, will be classified as hazardous waste and its shipment to non-OECD countries will be prohibited under the Waste Shipment Regulation. This aims to retain the necessary raw materials for battery recycling within the EU and to increase battery recycling in Europe.

– In the section on rules of origin:

It is stated that consultations will be held with the automotive sector and member states regarding the approach to preferential rules of origin. In particular, the Commission will consider defining specific rules of origin in trade defense instruments for the European automotive sector in order to deter companies from third countries that benefit from preferential regimes in the EU market from engaging in unfair trade practices and establishing production facilities.

It is emphasized that the Commission is currently imposing countervailing duties on electric vehicles imported from China to address unfair trade practices. If sufficient evidence is obtained indicating that these measures are being circumvented, the Commission is prepared to initiate an investigation. Additionally, the Commission is equally determined to utilize Trade Defense Instruments and the Foreign Subsidies Regulation to investigate unfair practices at later stages of the supply chain, including the battery and parts segments, if necessary. In this context, it is considered that potential Chinese investments in our country may be subject to scrutiny within the scope of circumvention of measures and the Foreign Subsidies Instrument.

Click here for the Commission's  Factsheet  on the plan.

Click here for the Commission's page regarding questions and answers on the topic.
